text: Thanks for coming in today. Let's start with a behavioral question. Tell me about a time when you had to deal with a difficult team situation. Sure. At my previous company, I was leading a team of five engineers. We had a tight deadline for a product launch, and two team members had a disagreement about the architecture. The situation was getting tense and affecting team morale. My responsibility was to resolve the conflict and keep the project on track. What I did was I set up individual meetings with both engineers to understand their perspectives. Then I facilitated a design review where we evaluated both approaches against our technical requirements. I proposed a compromise that incorporated the strengths of both designs. The result was that we shipped on time, and the team actually ended up with a better architecture than either original proposal. Both engineers felt heard and our team velocity improved by 20% the following sprint. That's a great example. Can you tell me about a time you failed at something? Yes. I once tried to implement a new testing framework across the entire engineering org without getting buy-in first. I just went ahead and started migrating tests. The result was pushback from other teams who felt blindsided. I learned that technical decisions need stakeholder alignment, not just technical merit. Now I always start with a proposal document and get feedback before making changes. Good. One more. Describe a situation where you had to learn something quickly. When I joined my current role, I had to learn Kubernetes in two weeks to deploy our new microservices. I set up a personal cluster, worked through tutorials, and paired with our DevOps engineer. Within three weeks I was able to independently deploy and debug services. I even created an internal guide that the rest of the team now uses.
